summary: Gruber responds to Jason Fried's argument about why copying 37signals (or anyone) is misguided, because copiers can't see the invisible work behind design decisions. He argues that 37signals' products work perfectly if you think like Jason Fried, which is exactly why knock-offs fail — they copy the surface without the underlying vision. The real lesson isn't to replicate features but to adopt the attitude of building what you yourself want. Gruber contends that designing for 'everyone' rarely works, and the key ingredient in successful apps is creators building something they themselves love.
key_points: Copying a product's surface fails because the reasoning behind design decisions is invisible to the copier
37signals' strength is building exactly what they want rather than guessing what faceless customers might want
Products designed for 'everyone' rarely succeed — it's better to build something that works really well for some people
The truly copyable ingredient in successful products is the attitude of creators building what they themselves love
money_quotes: The problem is that the work on the original is invisible. The copier doesn't know why it looks the way it looks or feels the way it feels or reads the way it reads.
A common knock against 37signals's products is that they work perfectly — if you happen to think exactly like Jason Fried.
They didn't start with what customers wanted, or with what existing project management software looked like, or by trying to guess what some group of faceless others would want.
They designed and built what they themselves wanted, under the assumption that there were some number of other people who would want the same thing.
Rather than trying to build things that work OK for everyone, they're building things that work really well for some people.

The key ingredient I see in successful apps, Mac or web — and, really, in creative commercial endeavors of any kind — is that the creators are building something they themselves love. That's what you should copy.
key_insight: The thing worth copying from successful products isn't their features or interface but the attitude of building something the creators themselves genuinely love and want to use.
